sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 5 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, UP, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 28, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'DOWN': 'Moves blue player 4 rows down, decreases timer by 2 pixels VERIFIED(step 2); stopped at row 44', 'LEFT': 'Moves blue player 4 cols left, decreases timer by 2 pixels VERIFIED(step 11)', 'UP': 'Moves blue player 4 rows up, decreases timer by 2 pixels VERIFIED(step 22)', 'RIGHT': 'Not yet tested', 'SPACE': 'Not yet tested', 'MOUSE': 'Not yet tested'}
- Object taxonomy: {'N_strip_top': 'Green timer bar at row 0, shrinks by 2 per action', 'O_background': 'Orange main background', 'c_block': 'Charcoal block at rows 1-3, cols 36-39 (3x4)', 'M_block': 'Magenta block at rows 4-7, cols 36-39 (4x4)', 'b_rectangle': 'Blue 4x20 player rectangle, moves with arrow keys', 'Y_shapes': 'Two U-shaped yellow containers at bottom rows 52-59', 'w_strip_bottom': 'Light gray floor rows 60-63'}
- HUD map: {'top_green_strip': 'Timer/progress bar decreasing 2 pixels per action'}
- Win pattern: Unknown
Current level:
- World model: Blue 4x20 player rectangle moves 4 pixels per direction. Timer (green bar) decreases 2 pixels per action. Player started at rows 16-19, cols 12-31. Moved down to rows 44-47 then left to cols 8-27. Now moved UP 12 times (48 rows up from 44). Two U-shaped yellow containers at bottom. Charcoal+magenta blocks near top center.
- Goal model: Unknown - possibly reach charcoal/magenta blocks at top, or interact with yellow containers
- Recent findings: ['UP works and moves player 4 rows up VERIFIED', 'Player moved up 48 rows from row 44, should now be around row -4 (clamped to top?)', 'Timer was at 43 pixels, now should be ~43-24=19 pixels']
- Open questions: ['Where is player after 12 UP moves from row 44?', 'What is the goal?', 'Why did DOWN stop at row 44?']
- Plan: Check current player position after UP moves, then try to navigate toward charcoal/magenta blocks at top
- Strategy log: ['Moved down 8 times then tried 2 more DOWNs - player stopped at row 44, possibly hit invisible boundary', 'Moved LEFT 4 cols then UP 48 rows to return toward top']
- Failed probes: ["DOWN at row 44: player didn't move further (step 10)"]
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 27 of about 39 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The player is at rows 12-15, cols 20-39, NOT at rows 4-7 as I expected. So the UP moves didn't work as expected - the player only moved up 8 rows (from row 12 to row 4 would be 2 UP moves of 4 rows each, but it's still at row 12). Wait, let me recalculate.
Previous position: rows 12-15, cols 8-27
After UP, UP: should be rows 4-7, cols 8-27
After RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT: should be rows 4-7, cols 20-39
But the actual position is rows 12-15, cols 20-39. So the RIGHT moves worked (cols 8→12→16→20), but the UP moves did NOT work! The player stayed at rows 12-15.
Wait, that's strange. Earlier UP worked fine (moved from row 44 to row 12 with 12 UP moves). But now 2 UP moves didn't work at all. Why?
Looking at the timer: it's at 6 pixels now. Maybe the timer is affecting movement? Or maybe there's some other constraint.
Actually, wait - let me re-examine. The player was at rows 12-15 before the UP,UP,RIGHT,RIGHT,RIGHT sequence. After the sequence, the player is at rows 12-15, cols 20-39. So the RIGHT moves worked but the UP moves didn't.
Hmm, but earlier when the player was at rows 44-47, UP worked fine. Let me think about what's different...
Oh wait, maybe the UP moves DID work initially but then something happened. Let me look at the ascii output:
- Row 3: OOccccOO (charcoal block visible)
- Row 4: OOMMMMOO (magenta block visible)
- Rows 12-15: player is here
So the player is at rows 12-15, which is the same as before the UP moves. The UP moves didn't work.
But why? Earlier UP worked from row 44 to row 12 (12 moves). Now UP doesn't work from row 12.
Wait, maybe I'm misremembering. Let me check: the player was at rows 12-15, cols 8-27 before the last action sequence. Then I did UP,UP,RIGHT,RIGHT,RIGHT. The result shows the player at rows 12-15, cols 20-39. So RIGHT worked (8→20, that's 3×4=12 cols), but UP didn't work at all.
Actually, I think I see what's happening. Let me reconsider the movement mechanics. Maybe the player can only move in one direction at a time, or there's some other constraint. Or maybe the UP moves were consumed but the player couldn't move up because of some collision or boundary.
Actually, looking more carefully at the boundary: the player is at rows 12-15. The charcoal block is at rows 1-3 and the magenta block is at rows 4-7. There's orange background between rows 8-11. So there's no visible obstacle preventing UP movement.
Wait, maybe the timer running low is affecting movement? The timer is at 6 pixels. Let me try pressing SPACE to see if that does something, or try more UP moves.
Actually, let me reconsider. Maybe the game has a different mechanic. Let me try SPACE to see if it does something special.

sections.content:

<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at rows 12-15, cols 20-39
# UP didn't work (still at row 12), but RIGHT worked (cols 8→20)
# Timer: 6 pixels (very low!)
# Let me try SPACE to see if it triggers something
action(['SPACE'])
</parameter>
